name: Eurostar
highlight: Drops 75 m below the sea through the 50.5 km Channel Tunnel, 37.9 km of it under the seabed, the longest undersea stretch of any rail tunnel.

name: TGV inOui
highlight: France’s original 1981 LGV forms the first leg; today the TGV holds 320 km/h nearly all the way to the Mediterranean.

name: AVE
highlight: Scheduled at 310 km/h, Europe’s fastest timetable, this line flipped one of the world’s busiest air routes to rail.

name: AVE
highlight: Spain’s first AVE, built for Expo ’92 in standard gauge, the line that reset Spanish railways.

name: ICE Sprinter
highlight: The €10bn VDE 8 project: 26 tunnels and 37 viaducts through the Thuringian Forest halved the Berlin–Munich journey.

name: ICE
highlight: A roller-coaster alignment beside the A3 autobahn with 40 ‰ grades: only the most powerful ICE sets are cleared for it.

name: Frecciarossa
highlight: Italy’s flagship corridor: since 2009 the airlines’ share has collapsed, and the ETR 1000 is engineered for 360 km/h.

name: Eurostar (Thalys)
highlight: The crimson Thalys, folded into Eurostar in 2023, chains French, Belgian and Dutch high-speed lines into one dash.

name: LNER Azuma
highlight: The Flying Scotsman’s route since 1862: and where Mallard set the never-beaten steam record of 203 km/h in 1938.

name: Alfa Pendular
highlight: A tilting Pendolino leaning through the curves of the 19th-century Linha do Norte between Portugal’s two great cities.

name: EIP Pendolino
highlight: Rides Poland’s 1970s Central Main Line (CMK), engineered decades ahead of its time and now being upgraded to 250 km/h.

name: Railjet
highlight: Follows the old Orient Express alignment down the Danube plain: centre to centre faster than any flight.

name: SJ X2000
highlight: Sweden’s tilting flagship since 1990, sprinting the Western Main Line between the country’s two biggest cities.

name: EuroNight
highlight: Scandinavia’s night-train revival: across the Öresund while you sleep, into Germany by breakfast.

name: Santa Claus Express
highlight: Rolls out of Helsinki by night and crosses into Lapland by morning: sleepers and car carriers bound for the Arctic Circle.

name: Venice Simplon-Orient-Express
highlight: Belmond’s restored Art Deco sleepers of the 1920s–30s: black tie encouraged as the Alps slide past the champagne bar.

name: Glacier Express
highlight: “The world’s slowest express”: 291 bridges, 91 tunnels and the 2,033 m Oberalp Pass at an average of 36 km/h.

name: Bernina Express
highlight: A UNESCO World Heritage railway: 7 % grades with no rack, the Brusio spiral, and the highest rail crossing of the Alps at 2,253 m.

name: Bergensbanen
highlight: Northern Europe’s highest main line: over the bare Hardangervidda plateau at 1,237 m, through 182 tunnels.

name: West Highland Line
highlight: Regularly voted the world’s most scenic railway: around Rannoch Moor and over the 21-arch Glenfinnan Viaduct of film fame.

name: Tokaido Shinkansen
highlight: The original bullet train, opened for the 1964 Olympics: the world’s busiest high-speed line, with departures every few minutes past Mount Fuji.

name: Jinghu High-Speed
highlight: The world’s fastest scheduled trains: 350 km/h Fuxing sets covering 1,318 km in under four and a half hours.

name: Trans-Siberian Railway
highlight: The longest railway on Earth: 9,289 km, eight time zones and seven nights between Moscow and the Pacific.

name: Qinghai–Tibet Railway
highlight: The roof-of-the-world railway: 5,072 m over the Tanggula Pass on permafrost, with oxygen piped to every seat.

name: KTX
highlight: Korea’s TGV-derived flagship halved the Seoul–Busan run: today the corridor is one of the most intensively served on Earth.

name: Mumbai Rajdhani
highlight: India’s first Rajdhani: the overnight flagship linking the capital with the financial capital, now in bright Tejas livery.

name: Acela
highlight: America’s fastest train works the dense Northeast Corridor: 240 km/h bursts between five major downtowns.

name: California Zephyr
highlight: Two nights, seven states: through the Rockies’ canyons and over Donner Pass, widely called America’s most beautiful train ride.

name: Rocky Mountaineer
highlight: All-daylight luxury through the Canadian Rockies: glass-domed coaches, an overnight hotel stop and the Kicking Horse Pass.

name: Cusco–Machu Picchu
highlight: Down the Sacred Valley of the Incas beside the Urubamba rapids: the rails are the only road to Machu Picchu.

name: The Blue Train
highlight: A five-star hotel on rails: butlers, marble bathrooms and the Karoo semi-desert rolling past for 1,600 km.

name: Al Boraq
highlight: Africa’s first high-speed railway, named after a mythical steed, holds 320 km/h along the Atlantic between Tangier and Casablanca.

name: The Ghan
highlight: Named for the Afghan cameleers: 2,979 km straight through the Red Centre, completed to Darwin only in 2004.

name: Indian Pacific
highlight: Ocean to ocean across Australia, including the world’s longest railway straight: 478 dead-level kilometres on the Nullarbor.

name: TranzAlpine
highlight: Coast to coast across the Southern Alps: the Waimakariri gorges, Arthur’s Pass and the 8.5 km Otira Tunnel in one morning.

name: TGV inOui
highlight: The LGV Sud Europe Atlantique put Bordeaux’s vineyards two hours from Paris, and emptied the air shuttle.

name: TGV Est
highlight: Where a modified TGV hit 574.8 km/h in 2007: still the world rail speed record for steel wheels.

name: AVE
highlight: Madrid to the Mediterranean paella coast in 100 minutes aboard the duck-nosed “Pato”.

name: AVE
highlight: Splits from the Seville line at Córdoba and dives through the Sierra to the Costa del Sol.

name: Frecciarossa
highlight: Italy’s first true alta velocità line. Vesuvius on the horizon barely an hour after leaving Rome.

name: ICE
highlight: Germany’s busiest city pair rides an alignment opened in 1846, dead straight across the northern plain.

name: Caledonian Sleeper
highlight: The “Deerstalker Express”: fall asleep at Euston, wake up crossing Rannoch Moor with red deer at the window.

name: Flåmsbana
highlight: One of the steepest adhesion railways on Earth: 864 m down to the fjord in 20 km, waterfalls at the window.

name: Rhine Valley Line
highlight: Castle after castle past the Lorelei rock: the left bank of the Rhine is one long UNESCO postcard.

name: Semmeringbahn
highlight: The world’s first true mountain railway (1854) and a UNESCO site: 16 viaducts built before the age of dynamite.

name: Belgrade–Bar Railway
highlight: 254 tunnels, 435 bridges and the vertigo-inducing Mala Rijeka viaduct. Yugoslavia’s engineering swan song.

name: Nightjet
highlight: The founding route of the Nightjet brand: over the Arlberg while you sleep, Alps at both ends.

name: Gotthard Express
highlight: Threads the 57.1 km Gotthard Base Tunnel: the longest and deepest railway tunnel on the planet.

name: Arctic Circle Train
highlight: Crosses the Arctic Circle bound for the aurora: the Iron Ore Line’s fjord finale into Narvik is pure drama.

name: Sapsan
highlight: The “Peregrine Falcon” races the dead-straight 1851 Nikolaev alignment between Russia’s two capitals.

name: EIP Pendolino
highlight: Capital to the Baltic amber coast, Gdańsk’s Hanseatic old town, in under three hours.

name: Railjet
highlight: Follows the Habsburg Empire’s first steam railway of 1839. Prague, Brno and Vienna strung like pearls.

name: EN Ister
highlight: Retraces the original 1883 Orient Express alignment through Transylvania’s castles and the Carpathian passes.

name: Hellenic InterCity
highlight: Past Thermopylae and under Mount Olympus. Greece’s spine line, now run by tilting Italian arrows.

name: InterCity
highlight: Ireland’s premier line since the Famine years: green midlands all the way to the Rebel City.

name: Dovrebanen
highlight: Over the Dovrefjell plateau where wild musk oxen roam: mountain birch, snow fences and midnight sun.

name: Avanti West Coast
highlight: Tilting Pendolinos sway through the Lune Gorge and over Shap summit on Britain’s busiest mixed-traffic artery.

name: GoldenPass Line
highlight: Lake Geneva’s riviera to Lucerne over the Brünig Pass — two trains: the gauge-changing GPX to Interlaken Ost, then zb’s Luzern–Interlaken Express.

name: Jungfraubahn
highlight: Tunnels INSIDE the Eiger to Europe’s highest station at 3,454 m, the “Top of Europe” since 1912.

name: Hokuriku Shinkansen
highlight: Over the Japanese Alps to the samurai gardens of Kanazawa: copper-and-blue E7s built for deep snow country.

name: Hayabusa
highlight: Japan’s fastest trains dive beneath the sea through the 53.9 km Seikan Tunnel to reach Hokkaido.

name: Sanyo Shinkansen
highlight: The Inland Sea corridor past Himeji Castle and Hiroshima, and the home turf of the needle-nosed 500 Series.

name: Kyushu Shinkansen
highlight: Down the volcanic spine of Kyushu toward Sakurajima’s smoking cone: mostly inside tunnels, all speed.

name: Jingguang High-Speed
highlight: The longest high-speed line on Earth: 2,298 km from the Yellow River plain to the Pearl River Delta.

name: Jingha High-Speed
highlight: Cold-proof Fuxing sets built for −40 °C sprint to the ice-festival city of Harbin in Manchuria.

name: Shanghai Maglev
highlight: The fastest commercial train on the planet: 431 km/h with no wheels, floating on magnets.

name: Lanxin High-Speed
highlight: The world’s longest desert high-speed line: wind-walled against Gobi gales on the old Silk Road.

name: THSR
highlight: Shinkansen technology’s first export: the length of Taiwan’s west coast in ninety minutes.

name: Darjeeling Toy Train
highlight: A two-foot-gauge UNESCO marvel looping up to 2,200 m: steam engines older than the tea gardens they pass.

name: Kalka–Shimla Railway
highlight: 102 tunnels and 864 bridges to the Raj’s summer capital: a UNESCO line where the guard still waves flags.

name: Konkan Railway
highlight: Monsoon country: 2,000 bridges and 91 tunnels down the palm-fringed cliffs of India’s west coast.

name: Sri Lanka Main Line
highlight: Through tea country over the Nine Arches Bridge: many call Kandy to Ella the world’s most beautiful ride.

name: Chiang Mai Express
highlight: Thailand’s classic sleeper: dinner past Ayutthaya’s temples, dawn in the teak hills of Lanna.

name: Eastern & Oriental Express
highlight: Cream-and-green carriages gliding past rubber plantations: colonial-era glamour from the Lion City to Siam.

name: Reunification Express
highlight: Two nights along the South China Sea: the Hải Vân Pass clings to cliffs the road never dared.

name: Whoosh
highlight: Southeast Asia’s first high-speed railway: volcano views at 350 km/h across the Parahyangan highlands.

name: Laos–China Railway
highlight: Punched through jungle karst in 167 tunnels, it gave landlocked Laos its first real railway.

name: Afrosiyob
highlight: Central Asia’s only high-speed rail. Spanish Talgos racing to the turquoise domes of the Silk Road.

name: YHT
highlight: From the Bosphorus across the Anatolian steppe. Türkiye’s white Velaros bridge two continents’ doorstep.

name: Haramain High-Speed
highlight: Links Islam’s two holiest cities at 300 km/h through the desert: built to move millions of Hajj pilgrims.

name: Trans-Mongolian
highlight: The Trans-Mongolian’s Gobi crossing: gers, camels, and a bogie change at the Chinese border, where every coach is jacked up for the narrower gauge.

name: Empire Builder
highlight: Two nights tracing the Great Northern’s empire: skirting Glacier National Park along the Montana Rockies.

name: Coast Starlight
highlight: Cascade volcanoes, Bay fog and a hundred miles of Pacific surf from the parlour car’s armchairs.

name: Brightline
highlight: America’s first private intercity railway in a century: bright yellow, 200 km/h through the palms.

name: The Canadian
highlight: Four nights in 1955 stainless-steel dome cars. Ontario lakeland, prairie infinity, then the Rockies at dawn.

name: El Chepe
highlight: Sea level to 2,400 m through Copper Canyon: 37 bridges and 86 tunnels in country deeper than the Grand Canyon.

name: Tren Maya
highlight: A brand-new loop through the Yucatán linking Mayan pyramids. Chichén Itzá to Palenque by rail.

name: Tren a las Nubes
highlight: “Train to the Clouds”: zigzags and spirals up the Andes to the La Polvorilla viaduct at 4,220 m.

name: Serra Verde Express
highlight: Down the Serra do Mar through Brazil’s Atlantic rainforest: an 1885 line of viaducts pinned to green cliffs.

name: Denali Star
highlight: Twelve hours of wilderness with Denali filling the dome windows, flag stops still serve trailside cabins.

name: Andean Explorer
highlight: South America’s first luxury sleeper: over a 4,319 m pass and along Lake Titicaca under Andean stars.

name: Rovos Rail
highlight: “The Pride of Africa”: teak-panelled suites through the bushveld to the smoke that thunders.

name: Nile Valley Sleeper
highlight: Overnight up the Nile past Luxor’s temples: wake to feluccas and the First Cataract at Aswan.

name: Al Atlas
highlight: Across the Haouz plain to the red city below the High Atlas, the classic gateway to the souks.

name: Madaraka Express
highlight: Kenya’s standard-gauge flagship crosses Tsavo on elevated spans built so elephants pass underneath.

name: Addis–Djibouti Railway
highlight: Africa’s first fully electrified cross-border railway runs from the Ethiopian highlands to the Red Sea in a day.

name: Melbourne XPT
highlight: Australia’s InterCity 125 cousin runs the overnight between its two biggest rivals, cricket ashes optional.

name: Spirit of Queensland
highlight: A tilting train with lie-flat “railbeds” running the Coral Sea coast toward the Great Barrier Reef.

name: Northern Explorer
highlight: Volcano country: the Raurimu Spiral climbs to Tongariro’s plateau, Ruapehu steaming beyond the glass.

name: Settle–Carlisle Line
highlight: England’s highest main line crests Ais Gill at 356 m after striding across the 24-arch Ribblehead Viaduct, built by navvies camped on Batty Moss.

name: Night Riviera
highlight: Sleeping cars have run west from Paddington since 1877; today’s train hugs Brunel’s storm-lashed Dawlish sea wall to the far tip of Cornwall.

name: Enterprise
highlight: Jointly run from both capitals since 1947, it rolls over the 1855 Boyne Viaduct at Drogheda and crosses the invisible Irish border without a pause.

name: Nordlandsbanen
highlight: Norway’s longest railway: 729 km of fjordside, forest and bare Saltfjellet plateau, and the country’s only line across the Arctic Circle.

name: Rauma Line
highlight: Corkscrews down Romsdalen past the 1,100 m Trollveggen, Europe’s tallest vertical cliff, via the Stavem spiral tunnel and stone Kylling Bridge.

name: Linha do Douro
highlight: Clings to the Douro’s waterline for some 100 km on ledges blasted out in the 1880s, passing UNESCO-listed port-wine terraces at Régua and Pinhão.

name: Euromed
highlight: Talgo tilting sets swap the Madrid high-speed line for the classic Mediterranean corridor at Camp de Tarragona, running within sight of the beaches.

name: Intercités de Nuit Paris–Nice
highlight: Relaunched in 2021 as heir to the fabled Train Bleu: board at Austerlitz, wake to the red Estérel cliffs and the sea glittering at Saint-Raphaël.

name: Schwarzwaldbahn
highlight: Robert Gerwig’s 1873 blueprint for mountain railways climbs about 650 m through 39 tunnels and two sweeping double loops high above Triberg.

name: Mariazellerbahn
highlight: Opened in 1907 and electrified with its own rare 6.5 kV 25 Hz current in 1911, Austria’s grandest narrow-gauge line crests at 892 m in the Gösing Tunnel.

name: European Sleeper
highlight: Crowdfunded start-up that gave the Low Countries their night train back: via The Hague and Amsterdam to Berlin and the Elbe valley to Prague.

name: Nightjet
highlight: First route to get ÖBB’s new-generation Nightjets in 2023. Mini Cabin pods for solo travellers and 230 km/h sleeper stock.

name: Kyiv–Warsaw Sleeper
highlight: Wartime lifeline to the EU: at the Bug border every carriage is jacked up and its bogies swapped from 1,520 to 1,435 mm gauge while passengers doze.

name: RegioJet Night Train
highlight: Open-access yellow night train since 2015: cheap couchettes out of Prague, over the Jablunkov Pass and under the dark High Tatras to Košice.

name: ICN Zagreb–Split
highlight: Tilting railcars lean through the bare karst of the Lika plateau below Velebit, then swoop down to the palm-fringed Adriatic at Split.

name: Bohinj Railway
highlight: Habsburg Transalpina of 1906: the 6.3 km Bohinj tunnel, the emerald Soča, and Solkan’s 85 m arch, the world’s longest stone rail span.

name: Prahova Valley Railway
highlight: Climbs the Prahova gorge past Peleș Castle at Sinaia to Predeal, Romania’s highest station at 1,032 m, under the Bucegi’s 2,500 m walls.

name: ZSSK InterCity
highlight: Up the Váh valley, then along the 1872 Košice–Bohumín railway: the High Tatras panorama, crowned by 2,655 m Gerlach, rolls past at Poprad.

name: WESTbahn
highlight: Austria’s first open-access challenger, running since 2011: 200 km/h Stadler KISS double-deckers that drove fares down on the Vienna–Salzburg run.

name: Soko
highlight: Serbia’s first 200 km/h railway, built with Chinese and Russian contractors. Novi Sad in 36 minutes since 2022, Subotica since October 2025.

name: Sunrise Express
highlight: Japan’s last regular sleeper; the Izumo half splits at Okayama and takes the Hakubi Line gorges to the sacred San’in coast by breakfast.

name: Tadami Line
highlight: Reopened in 2022 after 11 years of flood repairs; drifts over the mist-wreathed Tadami River bridges through Japan’s deepest snow country.

name: Hong Kong–Beijing High-Speed Sleeper
highlight: Fuxing sleeper EMUs leave Kowloon’s skyline at dusk and cross five provinces overnight, rolling into Beijing West before seven in the morning.

name: Alishan Forest Railway
operator: Alishan Forest Railway & Cultural Heritage Office
highlight: 762 mm trains climb 2,186 m in 71 km via the Dulishan spiral and summit switchbacks; fully reopened in 2024, 15 years after Typhoon Morakot.

name: Nilgiri Mountain Railway
highlight: India’s only rack railway: X-class steam pushes the blue coaches up 1-in-12 grades from palm plains to Ooty’s tea hills; UNESCO-listed in 2005.

name: Vande Bharat Express
highlight: First of India’s home-built semi-high-speed EMUs (2019), skimming the Gangetic plain from the capital to Hinduism’s holiest city in eight hours.

name: Death Railway
highlight: Built by Allied POWs and Asian labourers in 1942–43; trains still cross the River Kwai bridge and creep over the cliff-hugging Wang Pho viaduct.

name: KTM ETS
highlight: Metre gauge yet electric at 140 km/h, among the fastest narrow-gauge trains anywhere, racing past Ipoh’s karst hills to the Penang ferry quay.

name: Tulpar Talgo
highlight: Kazakh-assembled Talgo sets glide low over the steppe, tracking Lake Balkhash’s western shore all night between the old and new capitals.

name: Jerusalem High-Speed Link
highlight: Israel’s first electrified line tunnels and bridges through the Judean Hills to Yitzhak Navon station, 80 m below Jerusalem, in half an hour.

name: City of New Orleans
highlight: Immortalized in Steve Goodman’s 1971 song: overnight down the Mississippi Delta past Memphis, Yazoo City and the Louisiana bayous.

name: Southwest Chief
highlight: Heir to the Santa Fe Super Chief, “Train of the Stars”: crests 2,388 m Raton Pass on the old Santa Fe Trail.

name: Sunset Limited
highlight: America’s oldest train name, running since 1894: crosses the Pecos River High Bridge and the Chihuahuan Desert three times a week.

name: Pacific Surfliner
highlight: Heir to the 1938 San Diegan: surfboards ride free as double-deckers skim the sand at Del Mar and San Clemente.

name: Auto Train
highlight: Cars and motorcycles ride in autoracks behind the sleepers: often the world’s longest passenger train, nonstop Virginia to Florida.

name: Durango & Silverton
highlight: 1882 gold-rush narrow gauge: steam-hauled ever since, on the Highline ledge blasted 120 m above the Animas River gorge.

name: Ocean
highlight: North America’s oldest continuously named train, since 1904: overnight past the Matapédia salmon rivers and Chaleur Bay to the Atlantic.

name: Winnipeg–Churchill
highlight: Two nights across muskeg and permafrost to Churchill, the polar-bear-and-beluga town on Hudson Bay that no road reaches.

name: La Trochita
highlight: Theroux’s Old Patagonian Express: 750 mm gauge, 1922 steam locomotives and wood-stove-heated wooden coaches across the steppe.

name: Trem Vitória a Minas
highlight: Brazil’s busiest long-distance train: daily along the Rio Doce, sharing metre-gauge tracks with 200-plus-wagon iron-ore trains.

name: TAZARA
highlight: Mao-era China’s grandest aid project: the ‘Freedom Railway’ gave landlocked Zambia a copper route that bypassed white-ruled Rhodesia.

name: Dakar TER
highlight: West Africa’s first modern electric railway carried 22.6 million riders in 2024, flying past Dakar’s gridlock at 160 km/h.

name: Lagos–Ibadan Train Service
highlight: Nigeria’s first double-track standard-gauge line; every grand station bears a national figure’s name, from Wole Soyinka to Obafemi Awolowo.

name: Abuja–Kaduna Train Service
highlight: Nigeria’s first standard-gauge railway (2016) links the capital to Kaduna’s Rigasa terminus, far safer than the kidnap-plagued expressway.

name: Gautrain
highlight: South Africa’s 160 km/h Electrostars dive under Sandton’s bank towers, stitching Johannesburg to Pretoria in under an hour.

name: Kuranda Scenic Railway
highlight: Navvies hand-carved 15 tunnels and 37 bridges up Barron Gorge in the 1880s; trains still pause at the falls’ lookout above the rainforest.

name: The Overland
highlight: Running since 1887 as the Intercolonial Express, Australia’s oldest interstate rail journey now crosses the Wimmera wheat belt twice weekly.

name: Coastal Pacific
highlight: Pinched between the Kaikōura ranges and Pacific surf past fur-seal colonies, on a line the 2016 quake lifted bodily out of the sea.

name: Brisbane XPT
highlight: The overnight run still books twin sleepers as it corkscrews through the Border Loop spiral, blasted through rainforest ranges in 1932.

name: The Prospector
highlight: Australia’s fastest diesel railcars shadow the 1903 goldfields water pipeline across the Wheatbelt to Kalgoorlie’s Golden Mile.

name: Joetsu Shinkansen
highlight: Dives under the Tanigawa range into Kawabata’s snow country. Tokyo to metres-deep Echigo-Yuzawa powder and Niigata’s sake coast in under ninety minutes.

name: Akita Shinkansen
highlight: Crimson “Komachi” units ride the Hayabusa at 320 km/h to Morioka, then swap to single-track mountain rails past Lake Tazawa: a bullet train on a branch-line stage.

name: Yamagata Shinkansen
highlight: Japan’s first mini-shinkansen: beyond Fukushima the “Tsubasa” runs re-gauged valley rails below Zao’s slopes into cherry and soba country.

name: Nishi-Kyushu Shinkansen
highlight: Japan’s newest and shortest bullet line. The white-and-red “Kamome” skims Omura Bay to Nagasaki while politics still debates the missing link to Hakata.

name: Kurobe Gorge Railway
highlight: Dam-builders’ narrow-gauge torokko clinging to Japan’s deepest gorge: the full line to Keyakidaira returns in October 2026 after the Noto quake.

name: Hakone Tozan Railway
highlight: Japan’s oldest mountain railway hauls itself up 1-in-12.5 grades through three switchbacks, hydrangeas brush the windows each June.

name: Resort Shirakami
highlight: A rapid that idles on purpose: shamisen players aboard, the Sea of Japan breaking at Senjojiki, and UNESCO’s Shirakami beech forests behind the dunes.

name: Sagano Scenic Railway
highlight: A retired JR alignment above the Hozugawa rapids: twenty-five slow minutes of maples and gorge that the modern main line skips through tunnels.

name: Yufuin no Mori
highlight: Forest-green parlour cars with a buffet of Kyushu treats: over the Mino passes and along the Kusu valley to the boutique onsen town under Mount Yufu.

name: Saphir Odoriko
highlight: All-Green-Car luxury down the Izu coast: nose windows, a fresh noodle bar, and Sagami Bay filling the glass roof beyond Atami’s tunnels.

name: SL Gunma
highlight: Restored D51 498 “Degoichi” and C61 20 haul retro coaches up the Joetsu Line on weekends: steam echoing off the Tone gorge under Tanigawa’s wall.

name: Seven Stars in Kyushu
highlight: Japan’s first luxury cruise train: ten suites and a piano bar on a slow lap of Kyushu’s volcano country; berths routinely go to a lottery.

name: Twilight Express Mizukaze
highlight: Heir to the beloved Twilight Express: emerald art-deco suites and open observation decks drifting along the San’in coast’s empty beaches.

name: Limited Express Hida
highlight: Hybrid HC85s shadow the jade-green Hida River for hours of gorge: riverside onsen at Gero, morning markets at Takayama, then down to Toyama Bay.

name: Limited Express Azusa
highlight: The alpinists’ express. E353s bank through Katsunuma’s vineyards and the Suwa lake basin to Matsumoto’s black castle, gateway to the Japan Alps.

name: Limited Express Sonic
highlight: The blue “wonderland express”: tilting 883s reverse at Kokura and chase the coast past Nakatsu to the steaming hells of Beppu and Oita.

name: Spacia X
highlight: Tobu’s champagne-white flagship to Nikko’s shrine forest: a cockpit lounge, craft beer on draught, and Tokyo Skytree receding from the rear window.

name: Odakyu Romancecar
highlight: Front-row observation seats since 1957, when the SE set a world narrow-gauge speed record. Shinjuku to Hakone’s hot springs without a change.

name: KTX Honam
highlight: Korea’s second high-speed spine: across the Honam rice plain to Gwangju and Mokpo’s seafood quays, where ferries fan out to a thousand islands.

name: SRT
highlight: The upstart from Gangnam. SRT slips out of Suseo through the 50 km Yulhyeon Tunnel and undercuts KTX fares all the way to Busan.

name: KTX Gangneung Line
highlight: Built for the PyeongChang 2018 Games: under the Taebaek passes in a 22 km tunnel, out to Gangneung’s espresso beaches on the East Sea.

name: Baekdudaegan Canyon Train
highlight: White-and-crimson canyon cars crawl the upper Nakdong gorge at 30 km/h: stove-warmed in winter, windows flung open over rapids no road reaches.

name: South-Link Line
highlight: Taiwan’s last main line to be completed: 35 tunnels through the island’s southern spine to Duoliang’s celebrated platform above the Pacific.

name: Pingxi Line
highlight: A 1920s coal branch where railcars squeeze between Shifen’s shop-fronts as sky lanterns lift off: waterfalls and a cat village along the way.

name: Puyuma Express
highlight: Named for the Puyuma people: tilting trains lean through the Yilan curves, past Qingshui’s sheer sea cliffs, into the East Rift Valley’s rice paddies.

name: Mauritania Iron Ore Train
highlight: The world’s longest trains, up to 2.5 km of ore hoppers across the open Sahara, with a single battered passenger coach swaying at the back.

name: Benguela Railway Express
highlight: Atlantic surf to the Congolese border: the Chinese-rebuilt Benguela line’s weekly sleeper climbs 1,344 km through Huambo’s highlands to Luau.

name: Transcamerounais
highlight: Cameroon’s nightly lifeline: couchettes climb from Yaoundé’s hills to the Adamawa Plateau, railhead for the country’s wild north.

name: La Gazelle
highlight: Silenced by war in the Pool, rolling again since 2023: the Congo-Océan line dives through the Mayombe forest to the Atlantic at Pointe-Noire.

name: Algiers–Oran Express
highlight: Algeria’s premier inter-city: bimodal Coradias run the 1871 French-built mainline through the Chélif valley, Algiers to Oran in an afternoon.

name: Algiers–Constantine Express
highlight: East across the high plains of Sétif to Constantine, the “city of bridges” strung over the Rhumel gorge. Algeria’s grandest station approach.

name: Alexandria–Aswan Express
highlight: One seat, the whole of Egypt: from the Mediterranean up Africa’s oldest main line, waking to palms, feluccas and temples along the Nile to Aswan.

name: Al Atlas Night
highlight: Morocco’s night train, out of Tangier at 23:25, couchettes rolling south past Asilah and Casablanca to dawn at the foot of the Koutoubia in Marrakech.

name: Victoria Falls Overnight
highlight: Revived in October 2025: the 1904 line to the Falls leaves Bulawayo at dusk, skirts Hwange’s elephant country and arrives with the spray in sight.

name: Lézard Rouge
highlight: The Bey of Tunis’s 1910 saloon cars, freshly restored: creeping into the Selja gorge’s ochre cliffs, photo stops included, in phosphate country.

name: Tanzania SGR
highlight: East Africa’s first electric railway: 160 km/h from Dar’s Indian Ocean humidity to the capital in under four hours, opened 2024 and already packed.

name: Warri–Itakpe Train
highlight: Begun in 1987 and finished 33 years later: the standard-gauge line linking the Niger Delta’s creeks with the iron hills of Kogi State.

name: Puffing Billy Railway
highlight: Legs out the window over Monbulk Creek trestle: century-old narrow-gauge steam through Dandenongs fern gullies, Australia’s best-loved heritage line.

name: Spirit of the Outback
highlight: Up the coast to Rockhampton, then dead west into Matilda country: sleepers and the Tuckerbox diner, 1,325 km to the stockman’s capital.

name: The Gulflander
highlight: “From nowhere to nowhere” once a week: the 1891 Gulf line’s hollow steel sleepers shrug off every wet-season flood; the old railmotor just keeps going.

name: West Coast Wilderness Railway
highlight: The 1896 copper railway hauls itself up 1-in-16 grades on Abt rack cogs, steaming once more from Queenstown’s bare hills into the King River rainforest.

name: Taieri Gorge Railway
highlight: Out of Dunedin’s bluestone palace of a station and into the Taieri’s schist canyon, where wrought-iron viaducts from 1889 still carry every train.

name: Capital Connection
highlight: The Kāpiti Coast’s own express: one morning run to Wellington under Paekākāriki’s escarpment, home again each evening since 1991.

name: Pichi Richi Railway
highlight: The original Ghan route through Pichi Richi Pass: 1879 stonework, volunteer-run steam and the 1928 “Barwell Bull” railcar to quaint Quorn.

name: Mary Valley Rattler
highlight: A 1920s C17 barks out of Gympie across Deep Creek bridge: the 1915 fruit branch through the Mary Valley, saved twice and steaming on weekends.

name: The Inlander
highlight: Queensland’s grand old night train since 1953. Coral Sea to Cloncurry’s red plains and the smokestacks of Mount Isa, 977 unhurried kilometres.

name: The Westlander
highlight: Over the Toowoomba range by night and out across the Darling Downs: twice a week until the wheat gives way to mulga at Charleville.

name: Byron Bay Solar Train
highlight: The world’s first fully solar-powered train: a 1949 “red rattler” gliding three kilometres of resurrected line to Byron Bay’s beaches.

name: Great Southern
highlight: The Ghan’s summer sibling. Adelaide to Brisbane the long way round, with Grampians sunsets, Canberra and a Coffs Harbour beach stop en route.

name: Shanghai–Kunming HSR
highlight: China’s longest east–west high-speed line: 2,252 km from the Bund to the Yunnan plateau, vaulting Guizhou’s canyons on some of the world’s highest bridges.

name: Southeast Coastal HSR
highlight: D-trains skim 1,450 km of fishing ports, tea hills and sea bridges between the Yangtze and Pearl deltas. China’s window seat on the East China Sea.

name: Chengdu–Chongqing HSR
highlight: Fuxings at 350 km/h stitch the twin megacities of the southwest into 62 minutes: hotpot in Chengdu, riverside neon in Chongqing before it cools.

name: Xi’an–Chengdu HSR
highlight: The first bullet line across the Qinling: 127 km of tunnels under the range that divides north and south China, Terracotta Army to pandas in three hours.

name: Hainan Ring HSR
highlight: The world’s only circular high-speed railway: 653 km looping a tropical island past surf beaches, rocket launch pads and coconut groves.

name: Guangzhou–Hong Kong XRL
highlight: Under the Pearl River Delta in 47 minutes: dim sum in Guangzhou, and the harbour skyline rising over West Kowloon’s station roof by mid-morning.

name: Beijing–Zhangjiakou HSR
highlight: The world’s first autonomous 350 km/h railway, built for the 2022 Winter Games above Zhan Tianyou’s 1909 line: one station lies 102 m beneath the Great Wall.

name: Harbin–Dalian HSR
highlight: The world’s first alpine high-speed line: 921 km from Siberian-cold Harbin to the ice-free port of Dalian on EMUs winterised for −40 °C.

name: Guiyang–Guangzhou HSR
highlight: Eighty-three percent tunnel and bridge: 238 bores through Guizhou’s karst down to the Pearl Delta, four hours where the road once took a day and a night.

name: Lhasa–Nyingchi Railway
highlight: Oxygen-fed Fuxings trace the Yarlung Tsangpo at 3,500 m, crossing the great river 16 times between Lhasa and the peach orchards of Nyingchi.

name: Chengdu–Kunming Railway
highlight: The impossible mountain line of 1970: 427 tunnels and 991 bridges through the Daliangshan, where 5-yuan slow trains still double as the Yi villages’ market bus.

name: Southern Xinjiang Railway
highlight: All-night sleepers skirt the Tian Shan and the Taklamakan’s rim to Kashgar, the last great Silk Road bazaar before the Pamirs.

name: Arktika
highlight: Russia’s polar flagship crosses the Arctic Circle on the 1916 Murman railway: 35 hours north to a port where winter trains arrive in round-the-clock night.

name: Moscow–Kazan Double-Decker
highlight: Two storeys of berths slip out of Kazansky station at 23:08 and pull in below the Tatar kremlin by breakfast. Russia’s double-deck night-train boom, eastbound.

name: Red Arrow
highlight: Departing at 23:55 nightly since 1931 to the strains of ‘Hymn to the Great City’: the crimson Soviet flagship that outlived the state that built it.

name: Circum-Baikal Express
highlight: The Tsar’s ‘golden buckle’: 38 tunnels blasted into cliffs a step above Baikal’s water, once the Trans-Siberian’s costliest link, now a slow shoreline excursion.

name: Baikal–Amur Mainline
highlight: Brezhnev’s ‘Project of the Century’, the Trans-Siberian’s wilder northern twin: 3,800 km of permafrost, seven ranges and towns that exist only for the line.

name: Tbilisi–Batumi Intercity
highlight: Swiss double-deckers roll from the capital through Stalin’s Gori and the Imereti vineyards to palm-lined Batumi. Georgia crossed in five hours.

name: Baku–Tbilisi Night Train
highlight: Back after six years in May 2026: factory-fresh Stadler sleepers with showers and a VIP lounge leave the Caspian at night and wake above the Kura in Tbilisi.

name: Baku–Ganja Express
highlight: Double-deck KISS EMUs since 2021, sprinting the Kura steppe to Azerbaijan’s second city: the quickest thing on rails between the Caspian and the Lesser Caucasus.

name: Yerevan–Tbilisi Night Train
highlight: An every-other-night time capsule down the Debed gorge past Alaverdi’s copper smelters: extended each summer through to the beaches of Batumi.

name: Almaty–Tashkent Talgo
highlight: Central Asia’s new international Talgo, daily since December 2025: dinner leaving Almaty under the Tian Shan, lunch next day in Tashkent.

name: Tashkent–Khiva Night Train
highlight: Khiva had no railway until 2018: now a sleeper crosses the Kyzylkum desert overnight and draws up beside the mud-walled Itchan Kala at breakfast.

name: Issyk-Kul Summer Train
highlight: A summer-weekend crawl up the Boom Gorge to Issyk-Kul, the world’s second-largest alpine lake: panoramic cars bolted on for the mountain walls.

name: Ashgabat–Türkmenabat Night Train
highlight: Down the Tsarist Trans-Caspian mainline of 1886: overnight past Merv’s ruined oasis and the Karakum sands to the Amu Darya’s banks.

name: Gornergrat Bahn
highlight: Europe’s highest open-air railway climbs from Zermatt to 3,089 m: the Matterhorn framed in the window from the first curve to the summit ridge.

name: Pilatus Railway
highlight: The steepest rack railway on Earth: 48 % grades since 1889, with Locher’s horizontal cog wheels gripping a ladder no ordinary train could climb.

name: Rigi Railways
highlight: Europe’s first mountain railway (1871) still ratchets up from Lake Lucerne to the Queen of the Mountains. Mark Twain rode it and wrote home about it.

name: Brockenbahn
highlight: Metre-gauge steam still storms year-round to the 1,141 m Brocken. Goethe’s witches’ mountain, sealed off as a Soviet listening post until 1989.

name: Bayerische Zugspitzbahn
highlight: Germany’s highest railway: cogwheels from Garmisch past the Eibsee, then 4.5 km of tunnel to snowfields at 2,588 m below the country’s highest summit.

name: Centovalli Railway
highlight: Fifty-two km, 83 bridges and 31 tunnels between Piedmont and Ticino: chestnut forests, waterfalls and stone villages a hundred valleys deep.

name: Höllentalbahn
highlight: Germany’s steepest main line climbs Hell’s Valley at 55 ‰: through the Hirschsprung gorge and over the Ravenna viaduct into the high Black Forest.

name: Ffestiniog & Welsh Highland
highlight: The world’s oldest narrow-gauge railway company (1836): double-ended Fairlie steam from Caernarfon castle through Snowdonia to the slate town of Blaenau.

name: The Jacobite
highlight: Steam over the 21 arches of Glenfinnan on the 1901 Mallaig extension: the working train that became the Hogwarts Express, white sands of Morar below.

name: Snowdon Mountain Railway
highlight: Britain’s only public rack-and-pinion railway, Swiss-engineered in 1896: 950 vertical metres from Llanberis to the roof of Wales.

name: Train Jaune
highlight: The canary-yellow metre gauge of the Pyrenees: open-air cars over the Séjourné and Gisclard bridges to France’s highest station at 1,593 m.

name: Le Cévenol
highlight: Down the Allier gorges on viaducts stacked over a river with no road: the Massif Central crossing the TGV era forgot, kept alive by sheer beauty.

name: Ligne des Hirondelles
highlight: 36 tunnels and 18 viaducts swooping like its namesake swallows through the high Jura: stacked stone arches right above the rooftops of Morez.

name: Intercités de Nuit Paris–Briançon
highlight: France’s southern-Alps sleeper: lights out at Austerlitz, blinds up in the Durance valley, arrival under the Vauban walls of France’s highest city.

name: Cinque Terre Express
highlight: Village-hopping between tunnels where Liguria’s cliffs meet the sea. Monterosso, Vernazza, Corniglia, Manarola and Riomaggiore in half an hour.

name: Frecciarossa Milan–Venice
highlight: 300 km/h out of Milan, then Verona, Vicenza and Padua: finishing with the causeway glide across the lagoon to Santa Lucia’s doorstep on the Grand Canal.

name: Nightjet Munich–Rome
highlight: Board after dinner in Bavaria, wake to Tuscan hills: mini-cabins and wireless charging on ÖBB’s newest sleepers, crossing the Tauern by night.

name: Nightjet Zurich–Amsterdam
highlight: Asleep along the Rhine, awake on the IJ: the 2021 revival that re-linked the Alps to the canals, sliding past Cologne’s floodlit cathedral at midnight.

name: Kyiv–Lviv Intercity+
highlight: Launched for Euro 2012 and never stopped: the Hyundai-built Intercity+ keeps capital and Galicia linked across the steppe, through blackout and war.

name: Rantarata Coastal Line
highlight: Finland’s two capitals on the 1903 Rantarata: “coast line” by name, though the track runs mostly inland through granite cuttings and forest. Pendolino Plus sets joined the double-deck ICs in late 2025.

name: Lyntog Copenhagen–Aarhus
highlight: Zealand to Jutland with no ferry: an 8 km tunnel and the 6.6 km West Bridge across the Great Belt made Denmark one rail country in 1997.

name: Sørlandsbanen
highlight: Norway’s south-coast main line, completed in wartime 1944: inland through pine and moor, touching salt water only at Kristiansand and the Jæren flats.

name: Rørosbanen
highlight: The original 1877 inland route to Trondheim: diesel country through Østerdalen’s forests to Røros, the UNESCO copper town still timber-built and soot-dark.

name: Inlandsbanan
highlight: Two summer days up Sweden’s green spine: moose at the window and a trackside stop at the Arctic Circle, where everyone piles out for photographs.

name: Tren de Sóller
highlight: Varnished mahogany carriages from 1912 thread thirteen tunnels through the Tramuntana into Sóller’s orange-grove valley. Mallorca’s rolling time machine.

name: Crescent
highlight: Heir to the 1925 Crescent Limited. Manhattan to the French Quarter past Blue Ridge foothills, midnight Atlanta and a dawn crawl across Lake Pontchartrain.

name: Cardinal
highlight: Amtrak’s slowest way to Chicago and its prettiest: tri-weekly through Virginia horse country and the whitewater canyons of the New River Gorge.

name: Lake Shore Limited
highlight: Heir to the New York Central’s 1897 Water Level Route: up the Hudson at dusk, along Lake Erie by night, into Chicago with morning coffee.

name: Silver Meteor
highlight: The Seaboard streamliner that opened Florida’s diesel age in 1939: 28 hours from Penn Station to palm-lined Miami, still running nightly.

name: Texas Eagle
highlight: A Missouri Pacific name kept alive from the Loop to the Alamo: with through sleepers to Los Angeles, one of the longest runs on the Amtrak map.

name: Amtrak Cascades
highlight: Salish Sea beaches, Puget Sound ferries and Willamette farmland: the Pacific Northwest’s green corridor train, re-equipping with new Airo sets in 2026.

name: Adirondack
highlight: Hudson castles, Lake Champlain narrows and border-country vineyards: widely called the most scenic day train in the eastern United States.

name: Floridian
highlight: Born of a 2024 tunnel-works merger of two classic trains: one seat from Lake Michigan over the Alleghenies to Tampa and Miami, nearly two days aboard.

name: Grand Canyon Railway
highlight: Since 1901 the painless way to the South Rim: vintage Pullmans roll across pine forest and high prairie to a log depot steps from the abyss.

name: Cumbres & Toltec Scenic Railroad
highlight: America’s highest steam railroad, 1880 Rio Grande narrow gauge. K-36 Mikados storm 3,054 m Cumbres Pass and teeter along the Toltec Gorge rim.

name: Mount Washington Cog Railway
highlight: The world’s first mountain-climbing cog railway (1869): still shoving trains up a 37% grade to New England’s stormiest, windiest summit.

name: White Pass & Yukon Route
highlight: Blasted through gold-rush cliffs in 26 months (1898–1900): 900 m of climbing out of Skagway on cantilever bridges, then along Lake Bennett to the Yukon.

name: Pikes Peak Cog Railway
operator: Broadmoor Manitou & Pikes Peak Cog Railway
highlight: North America’s highest railway has climbed to the 4,302 m summit that inspired “America the Beautiful” since 1891; Swiss railcars took over the grade in 2021.

name: Skeena
highlight: Two daylight days from the Rockies to the Pacific. Mount Robson, totem-pole country at Hazelton and the Skeena’s misty canyons, overnighting in Prince George.

name: Polar Bear Express
highlight: No road reaches Moosonee. Ontario Northland’s lifeline train rolls 300 km of spruce and muskeg to tidewater on James Bay, canoes and pickups riding along.

name: Tren Interoceánico
highlight: The 1907 Tehuantepec route reborn in 2023: ocean to ocean across Mexico’s narrowest waist, from Pacific salt winds to the Gulf refineries.

name: El Insurgente
highlight: Mexico’s 160 km/h interurban tunnels out of the Valley of Toluca: since February 2026 running the full 58 km into Observatorio in the capital.

name: Panama Canal Railway
highlight: The world’s first transcontinental railroad (1855): an hour ocean-to-ocean beside the Canal locks, skimming causeways through Gatún Lake jungle.

name: Ferrocarril Central Andino
highlight: A handful of departures a year over the 4,783 m Galera tunnel: zigzags, 58 bridges and oxygen on tap aboard Earth’s second-highest railway.

name: PeruRail Titicaca
highlight: Ten leisurely hours across the altiplano from Cusco to Lake Titicaca: white-linen lunch and an open observation car over the 4,319 m La Raya pass.

name: Tren Patagónico
highlight: Friday nights out of Viedma: sleeper berths, a dining car and an onboard cinema crawl across the Río Negro steppe to the lakes of Bariloche.

name: Tren del Fin del Mundo
highlight: The southernmost railway on Earth: a 500 mm steam line laid by Ushuaia’s prison labourers in 1909, now puffing into Tierra del Fuego National Park.

name: Expreso del Sur
highlight: Tuesdays and Fridays out of Oruro: past Lake Poopó’s salt shores to Uyuni by night, then down the quebradas to the Argentine border at Villazón.

name: Tren Santiago–Chillán
highlight: South America’s fastest scheduled trains: 160 km/h EMUs launched in 2023 sprint down Chile’s Central Valley past vineyards and snow-capped volcanoes.

name: Buscarril Talca–Constitución
highlight: Chile’s last country branch line, a National Monument: a little railbus picks its way up the Maule river gorge to vineyards and hamlets no road reaches.

name: Trem do Corcovado
highlight: Brazil’s first electrified railway (1884): a rack train climbing 700 m through Tijuca rainforest to Christ the Redeemer above Rio.

name: Estrada de Ferro Carajás
highlight: One of the world’s great everyday long hauls: 16 hours through Amazon-edge towns, sharing rails with 300-plus-wagon iron-ore trains bound for Carajás.

name: Tren de la Sabana
highlight: Weekend steam across the Bogotá savanna: brass-era locomotives and papayera bands rolling to Zipaquirá’s underground Salt Cathedral.

name: Howrah Rajdhani
highlight: India’s first Rajdhani (1969): the all-air-conditioned overnight flagship racing the Grand Chord from the capital to Kolkata, dinner and bedrolls at your berth.

name: Bhopal Vande Bharat
highlight: India’s fastest timetabled train: a home-built Vande Bharat touching 160 km/h past Agra and Gwalior, capital to Bhopal in seven and a half hours.

name: Deccan Queen
highlight: Maharashtra’s beloved “Queen” since 1930. India’s first deluxe electric express, still climbing the monsoon-soaked Bhor Ghat with the network’s last dining car.

name: Matheran Hill Railway
highlight: A 1907 two-foot-gauge toy train corkscrewing 21 km up to a car-free hill station: 281 curves, the “One Kiss Tunnel”, and monkeys pacing the carriages.

name: Maharajas’ Express
highlight: IRCTC’s rolling palace: a week-long royal loop from Delhi through Ranthambore’s tigers, champagne breakfast beside the Taj, and evening prayers on the Ganges.

name: Island Express
highlight: Kerala’s workhorse since 1940: overnight from Bengaluru’s plateau down the palm-and-backwater coast to the subcontinent’s southernmost station at Kanniyakumari.

name: Golden Temple Mail
highlight: Born 1928 as the Frontier Mail to Peshawar: the Raj’s most glamorous train still runs daily, Mumbai to the Golden Temple’s city, 1,890 km up the map.

name: Green Line Express
highlight: Pakistan’s premium flagship since 2015: 22 hours up the Indus valley from Karachi’s sea air to Islamabad’s Margalla foothills, meals and bedding included.

name: Khyber Mail
highlight: Rolling since 1920 and named for the pass beyond Peshawar. Pakistan’s grand old mail train, two nights along the Indus, a lounge diner added in 2024.

name: Sonar Bangla Express
highlight: Bangladesh’s premier near-non-stop. Dhaka to the port city of Chattogram in under five hours aboard Indonesian-built carriages with airline-style catering.

name: Cox’s Bazar Express
highlight: Opened 2023 to the world’s longest natural beach: an overnight glide from Dhaka to a seashell-shaped terminus a rickshaw ride from the surf.

name: Janakpur Railway
highlight: Nepal’s only passenger railway, reborn in 2022: a crimson DEMU ferrying pilgrims across the border plain from Jaynagar to Sita’s temple city of Janakpur.

name: Ruhunu Kumari
highlight: Sri Lanka’s first intercity express (1955), so close to the Indian Ocean between Colombo and Galle that spray from the breakers reaches the footboards.

name: Yangon Circular Railway
highlight: A 46 km loop through Yangon life. Monks, hawkers and vegetable baskets share the ex-Japanese railcars, three unhurried hours for pocket change.

name: Bangkok–Vientiane Express
highlight: Since July 2024 the sleeper rolls straight across the Mekong Friendship Bridge, asleep past Ayutthaya, awake in Laos: the first through train from Bangkok into central Vientiane.

name: Hat Yai Special Express
highlight: Thailand’s southern flagship: 945 km down the Kra Isthmus past Hua Hin’s royal station and Surat Thani’s ferry piers, the sleeper gateway to Malaysia.

name: Royal Railway Southern Line
highlight: Weekend trains on Cambodia’s 1960s line to the sea, revived in 2016: through Takeo and Kampot’s pepper country to Sihanoukville’s beaches.

name: Ekspres Rakyat Timuran
highlight: Malaysia’s last sleeper: the overnight “Jungle Railway” through rainforest, limestone gorges and sleepy kampungs from Johor Bahru to the Thai border.

name: Argo Bromo Anggrek
highlight: Pride of Java’s north coast since 1997. Jakarta to Surabaya in under eight hours with two stops, now carrying KAI’s first Suite Class compartments.

name: Argo Wilis
highlight: The great trans-Java day train: curving around the Lebakjero horseshoe under Priangan’s volcanoes, then along the southern rice plains to Surabaya.

name: Bima
highlight: Indonesia’s original air-conditioned night train (1967): the “Biru Malam” still slips out of Jakarta at dusk and glides past Yogyakarta before dawn.

name: Tehran–Mashhad Express
highlight: Iran’s busiest corridor: five-star Fadak sleepers glide 926 km east past desert caravanserai country to Imam Reza’s shrine city of Mashhad.

name: Trans-Iranian Railway
highlight: The UNESCO-listed 1938 masterpiece paid for by taxes on tea and sugar: night trains spiral down the Zagros through a maze of tunnels to the Gulf plain.

name: Baghdad–Basra Night Train
highlight: Iraq back on rails: since 2014 a Chinese-built sleeper DMU hums south past Babylon and the Euphrates towns, Baghdad to Basra by breakfast.

name: Etihad Rail
highlight: The UAE’s first passenger railway, launched June 2026: 200 km/h out of Abu Dhabi and through Hajar mountain tunnels to Fujairah in 105 minutes.

name: Northeast Regional
highlight: Amtrak’s busiest train, over 12 million riders a year, racing the electrified Northeast Corridor at up to 125 mph without the Acela fare.

name: Vermonter
highlight: One of Amtrak’s longest daytime runs; trains reverse at Springfield to swing up the Connecticut River valley into Vermont’s Green Mountains.

name: Downeaster
highlight: Maine’s state-run link passed 10 million riders in December 2024, serving coastal Old Orchard Beach and the outlet town of Freeport.

name: Ethan Allen Express
highlight: In July 2022 it became the first passenger train to reach Burlington in 69 years, extending north from Rutland along Lake Champlain.

name: Maple Leaf
highlight: The only Amtrak–VIA joint train: passengers clear customs at Niagara Falls, where the line crosses the gorge beside the thundering cataract.

name: Pennsylvanian
highlight: Sweeps around Horseshoe Curve near Altoona: the 1854 engineering landmark that finally tamed the Allegheny grade.

name: Piedmont
highlight: North Carolina’s home-grown corridor: the state owns the trains, a fleet of rebuilt 1960s streamliner cars, running four round trips a day.

name: Heartland Flyer
highlight: Oklahoma’s only Amtrak train and the sole daily rail link into Texas, timed at Fort Worth to meet the Chicago–LA Texas Eagle.

name: Carolinian
highlight: At 704 miles the longest state-supported route in the Amtrak system, swapping its electric locomotive for a diesel at Washington.

name: Wolverine
highlight: Hits 110 mph on state-owned track between Kalamazoo and Dearborn: one of the few 100-mph corridors outside the Northeast.

name: Hiawatha
highlight: The Midwest’s busiest corridor: seven round trips a day linking downtown Chicago and Milwaukee in about 90 minutes.

name: San Joaquins
highlight: A Central Valley workhorse whose Thruway buses fan out to Yosemite, LA and the coast; over half of riders connect to a bus.

name: Capitol Corridor
highlight: Amtrak’s fourth-busiest route, along San Pablo Bay from Silicon Valley to the Sierra foothills; one daily train runs the full climb to Auburn.

name: Cass Scenic Railroad
operator: Mountain Rail WV · WV State Parks
highlight: Home to the world’s largest fleet of geared Shay steam locomotives, grinding up 11% grades to Bald Knob, 4,842 ft. West Virginia’s third-highest point.

name: Verde Canyon Railroad
highlight: Two 1953 FP7 diesels, once Alaska Railroad engines, thread a 680-ft curved tunnel and a red-rock canyon that shelters nesting bald eagles.

name: Napa Valley Wine Train
highlight: Guests wine and dine in restored early-1900s Pullman lounge cars behind vintage 1950s Alco diesels, rolling past Napa vineyards at a tasting pace.

name: Great Smoky Mountains Railroad
highlight: The spectacular train-wreck scene in The Fugitive (1993) was staged on this line; the wrecked locomotive still sits trackside near Dillsboro.

name: Georgetown Loop Railroad
highlight: To gain 640 ft between two towns just 2 miles apart, the narrow gauge spirals over itself on the Devil’s Gate High Bridge, an 1884 marvel rebuilt in 1984.

name: Skunk Train
highlight: Nicknamed the “Skunk” in 1925 for its reeking gas railcars, you smelled it before you saw it, still winding out of Fort Bragg through the coast redwoods.

name: Mount Hood Railroad
highlight: Climbs the orchard-filled Hood River valley toward 11,240-ft Mount Hood, reversing up one of just five railroad switchbacks still in regular US use.

name: VIA Rail Corridor
highlight: VIA’s new Siemens Venture trainsets made their Toronto debut on this Ottawa run in October 2024, cruising at up to 160 km/h.

name: VIA Rail Corridor
highlight: Rides the Grand Trunk main line of 1856, which once cut the Toronto–Montreal journey from five days to fourteen hours.

name: VIA Rail Corridor
highlight: The quickest run reaches Québec City’s château-style Gare du Palais in about 2 h 43 min, tracing the south shore of the St. Lawrence.

name: Agawa Canyon Tour Train
highlight: A 10-hour day trip 114 miles north into a wilderness canyon whose blazing autumn colours inspired Canada’s Group of Seven painters.

name: José Cuervo Express
highlight: Pours tequila and stages mariachi as it rolls through UNESCO-listed blue-agave fields to the spirit’s namesake town, Tequila.

name: Tren Francés
highlight: Named for the ex-French TEE coaches France sold Cuba in 2000; Chinese-built cars now run this 854 km island-spanning line, often only every few days.

name: Nariz del Diablo
highlight: To scale the sheer “Devil’s Nose” cliff the line zig-zags up a Z-shaped switchback, edging forward then reversing; reopened in August 2025 after five years shut.

name: Ferrocarril Ezequiel Zamora
highlight: Venezuela’s flagship modern railway bores through long mountain tunnels at 120 km/h to link Caracas with the Tuy-valley town of Cúa in just 31 minutes.

name: Tren a Mar del Plata
highlight: Rails reached the great Atlantic beach resort in 1886; today Chinese-built coaches cross 400 km of pampas from Constitución, packed with summer crowds.

name: Tren de las Sierras
highlight: Revived in 2007, this 1889 line climbs the Punilla valley past Lake San Roque, carrying refurbished ex-Portuguese railcars up into the Sierras de Córdoba.

name: AFE Tacuarembó–Rivera
highlight: Uruguay’s last passenger train. Swedish-built Y1 railcars rattle five days a week through the northern hills to the Brazilian border at Rivera.

name: Express InterCity Premium
highlight: Poland’s fastest train, hitting 200 km/h on the dead-straight Central Rail Line (CMK) engineered for high speed back in the 1970s.

name: Express InterCity
highlight: Historic trunk line on the Paris–Berlin–Warszawa–Moscow corridor; the same rails carry the Berlin-Warszawa-Express onward to Germany.

name: IC Przemyślanin
highlight: Crosses Poland to Przemyśl, the southeastern gateway where standard gauge meets Ukraine’s broad gauge, handover point for Kyiv-bound trains.

name: Kraków–Zakopane
highlight: Climbs from Kraków into the Tatra Mountains on the rebuilt line to Zakopane, Poland’s highest town and winter-sports capital.

name: SC Pendolino
highlight: Czechia’s flagship tilting Pendolino links Prague with Moravia’s industrial capital Ostrava via the cathedral city of Olomouc.

name: RegioJet
highlight: Yellow open-access trains with steward service and free coffee. RegioJet broke the state railway’s grip on the Prague–Brno run.

name: Praha–Cheb Express
highlight: Heads west through Plzeň, birthplace of Pilsner lager, to the spa-country town of Cheb near the German border.

name: Zubačka Rack Railway
highlight: Czechia’s only cog railway, climbing the Jizera Mountains on an Abt rack at a gradient of 58‰, a national record.

name: Tatra Electric Railway (TEŽ)
highlight: Metre-gauge electric cars climb to the High Tatra resort of Štrbské Pleso at 1,320 m, Slovakia’s highest railway station.

name: Balaton InterCity
highlight: Summer expresses to Siófok on the south shore of Lake Balaton, central Europe’s largest lake and Hungary’s beach playground.

name: InterCity Debrecen
highlight: Hourly InterCity across the Great Hungarian Plain to Debrecen, the nation’s second city, on the 160 km/h eastern main line.

name: InterCity Pécs
highlight: InterCity to Pécs, a sun-warmed city of Roman tombs and an Ottoman mosque near the Croatian border in southern Hungary.

name: Children’s Railway (Gyermekvasút)
highlight: A 760 mm line through the Buda Hills staffed almost entirely by children aged 10–14: a Young Pioneer legacy still running today.

name: InterRegio Cluj
highlight: The long haul from Bucharest over the Carpathians via Brașov and medieval Sighișoara to Cluj-Napoca, heart of Transylvania.

name: InterRegio Timișoara
highlight: Follows the Danube past the Iron Gates gorge, then climbs to Timișoara, the Banat city where Romania’s 1989 revolution ignited.

name: Mocănița Vaser Valley
highlight: The world’s last steam-hauled forestry railway still logging, chuffing up the wild Vaser gorge in Maramureș toward the Ukrainian border.

name: InterRegio Sibiu–Brașov
highlight: Runs under the Făgăraș Mountains, Romania’s highest, along the Olt valley between the Saxon citadels of Sibiu and Brașov.

name: BDŽ Fast Train Plovdiv
highlight: Bulgaria’s busiest intercity corridor, being rebuilt for 200 km/h, tying the capital to Plovdiv, one of Europe’s oldest cities.

name: BDŽ Fast Train Varna
highlight: Crosses the Balkan range and the Danubian plain to Varna, Bulgaria’s summer capital on the Black Sea coast.

name: BDŽ Fast Train Burgas
highlight: Runs via Plovdiv and Stara Zagora to Burgas, the port at the heart of Bulgaria’s southern Black Sea beaches.

name: Rhodope Narrow-Gauge
highlight: Bulgaria’s last 760 mm line threads 35 tunnels up to Avramovo, the highest railway station in the Balkans at 1,267 m.

name: Intercity+
highlight: Ukraine’s flagship Intercity+ express, launched for Euro 2012, races across the steppe from Kyiv to Kharkiv via Poltava.

name: Intercity+
highlight: Runs south from Kyiv through the black-earth farmland via the Znamianka junction to Odesa, the grand imperial port on the Black Sea.

name: Intercity+
highlight: Fast Intercity+ from Kyiv to Dnipro, the rocket-building metropolis on the mighty Dnieper in Ukraine’s industrial east.

name: Minsk–Brest Business Class
highlight: Follows the old Moscow–Warsaw corridor to Brest, the fortress city on the Bug where Belarus’s broad gauge meets the EU frontier.

name: Minsk–Gomel Regional Express
highlight: Links the capital with Gomel, Belarus’s second city near the Ukrainian border and the fringe of the Chernobyl zone.

name: Prietenia
highlight: Overnight Friendship sleeper whose coaches are jacked up for a bogie swap at Ungheni. Soviet 1,520 mm broad gauge to European standard.

name: Chișinău–Iași Cross-Border
highlight: A symbolic cross-Prut service reopened in 2021, switching from Moldova’s broad gauge to Romania’s standard gauge at the Ungheni frontier.

name: Zhengzhou–Xi’an HSR
highlight: One of China’s first mountain bullet lines, boring through the loess hills of the Yellow River valley to link Zhengzhou with the ancient capital Xi’an in about two hours.

name: Datong–Xi’an HSR
highlight: Runs the length of Shanxi from the Datong grottoes past walled Pingyao, crossing the Yellow River into Shaanxi to reach Xi’an.

name: Nanning–Guangzhou HSR
highlight: Follows the Xijiang (West River) valley out of Guangxi down to the Pearl River Delta, linking Nanning with Guangzhou.

name: Zhengzhou–Chongqing HSR
highlight: A 1,068 km route crossing the Funiu and Daba ranges, cutting Zhengzhou–Chongqing from about eight hours to under four and a half.

name: Fuzhou–Xiamen HSR
highlight: China’s first 350 km/h cross-sea railway, vaulting three bays on the Taiwan Strait coast to link Fuzhou and Xiamen in 55 minutes.

name: Jinan–Qingdao HSR
highlight: A 350 km/h dash across Shandong from the provincial capital Jinan to the seaside beer city of Qingdao.

name: Hefei–Fuzhou HSR
highlight: Nicknamed China’s most beautiful high-speed line, curving past Mount Huangshan and the Wuyi Mountains through Jiangnan tea country.

name: Shitai HSR
highlight: Burrows under the Taihang range through the 27.8 km Taihang Tunnel, once China’s longest railway tunnel, to join Hebei and Shanxi.

name: Guiyang–Nanning HSR
highlight: 350 km/h through Guizhou–Guangxi karst on a line that is mostly bridge and tunnel, passing the Libo World Heritage limestone scenery.

name: Nanning–Kunming HSR
highlight: Crosses the folded uplands and Nanpan River gorges between Guangxi and Yunnan’s capital, Kunming.

name: Chengdu–Guiyang HSR
highlight: Billed as the first high-speed railway through such deeply folded karst, spanning Jinsha River gorges on some of the world’s highest rail bridges.

name: Yinchuan–Xi’an HSR
highlight: The first bullet line to reach Ningxia, climbing the Loess Plateau and Ziwuling forests from Yellow River Yinchuan to Xi’an.

name: Zhengzhou–Jinan HSR
highlight: A fast diagonal across the North China Plain that crosses the Yellow River to knot together two provincial capitals.

name: Baoji–Lanzhou HSR
highlight: The final link of the east–west corridor to Lanzhou, over 90% bridge and loess tunnel, tracing the old Silk Road past Tianshui.

name: Chengdu–Ya’an Railway
highlight: The first completed stretch of the epic Sichuan–Tibet railway, heading west from Chengdu along the old Tea-Horse Road toward the plateau.

name: Lhasa–Shigatse Railway
highlight: A high-altitude spur off the Qinghai–Tibet line, tunnelling through the mountains to trace the Yarlung Tsangpo canyon to Tibet’s second city.

name: Dali–Lijiang Railway
highlight: Climbs from Dali’s Erhai Lake toward the Jade Dragon Snow Mountain and the old town of Lijiang, nearly all on bridges and through tunnels.

name: Erdenet Railway
highlight: Overnight sleeper north from the capital to the Soviet-built copper city of Erdenet, branching off the Trans-Mongolian near Darkhan.

name: Zamyn-Üüd Express
highlight: The domestic overnight across the Gobi Desert from Ulaanbaatar to the Chinese-border town of Zamyn-Üüd, halting at Choir and Sainshand.

name: Tulpar-Talgo
highlight: A Spanish-built Talgo hauled across the Kazakh steppe between Almaty and the southern city of Shymkent via Taraz.

name: Afrosiyob
highlight: Central Asia’s fastest train, a Talgo 250 racing the Kyzylkum steppe to link Tashkent and Samarkand with Silk Road Bukhara.

name: Uzbekistan Express
highlight: Electrified line into the crowded Fergana Valley through the 19.2 km Kamchik Tunnel, Central Asia’s longest, ending reliance on transit via Tajikistan.

name: Trans-Caspian Express
highlight: The historic Trans-Caspian line along the Karakum Desert’s edge, from Ashgabat to Turkmenistan’s lone Caspian port at Türkmenbaşy.

name: Dushanbe–Kulob Express
highlight: A weekly train made possible only in 2016, when a Chinese-built link finally joined Dushanbe to Tajikistan’s once-isolated southern railway.

name: Zhanghu HSR
highlight: Crosses the Inner Mongolian plateau via Ulanqab; linked to the Beijing–Zhangjiakou line it put Hohhot about two hours from Beijing.

name: ICE
highlight: Races the length of the North German Plain; the dead-straight Hanover–Berlin racetrack, partly laid on old border land, lets ICE 4 sets hold 250 km/h.

name: ICE
highlight: Sprints down the 300 km/h Nuremberg–Ingolstadt high-speed line, one of Germany’s fastest, on gradients so steep that freight trains are banned.

name: ICE
highlight: Runs the Hanover–Würzburg route, Germany’s first Neubaustrecke of 1991, boring through a chain of long tunnels under the hills of Hesse.

name: ICE
highlight: Joins the political and banking capitals via Kassel and the Fulda gap, the Velaro D holding 250 km/h over the wooded ridges of northern Hesse.

name: ICE
highlight: Opened in December 2022, the Wendlingen–Ulm line tunnels under the Swabian Jura and cut the Stuttgart–Ulm leg to just 42 minutes.

name: ICE
highlight: Crosses Germany from end to end, Berlin to the Swiss border, finishing down the Upper Rhine past Freiburg to Basel in about seven hours.

name: ICE International
highlight: Upgraded to new ICE 3neo trains in late 2025, replacing loco-hauled Intercitys on the roughly six-hour cross-border run to the Dutch capital.

name: ICE International
highlight: ICE 3M multi-system sets leave Amsterdam and, past Cologne, dive onto the 300 km/h Cologne–Rhine/Main line with its motorway-style gradients.

name: Moselstrecke
highlight: Follows the wine-terraced Moselle past Cochem, crossing the river on the historic two-tier Alf–Bullay bridge below Germany’s steepest vineyards.

name: Rübelandbahn
highlight: Heritage steam storms a fierce Harz grade once worked by rack rail; the 1926 tank engine 95 027, the Bergkönigin, still climbs to the caves at Rübeland.

name: Molli
highlight: A rare 900 mm-gauge steam line running since 1886, Molli puffs right down the middle of Bad Doberan’s tree-lined avenue on its way to the Baltic shore.

name: Rasender Roland
highlight: Nicknamed the Racing Roland, this 750 mm-gauge steam railway has linked Rügen’s white-sand Baltic resorts of Binz, Sellin and Göhren since 1895.

name: Zittauer Schmalspurbahn
highlight: 750 mm-gauge steam climbs from Zittau to the sandstone crags of the Zittau Mountains, serving the pilgrimage village of Oybin since 1890.

name: Intercity
highlight: The northbound Intercity crosses the young Flevoland polders on the Hanzelijn — the Almere–Lelystad stretch past the Oostvaardersplassen wetlands was voted the Netherlands’ most beautiful train journey by NS passengers in 2016 — then skirts the low Hondsrug ridge to Groningen in a little over two hours.

name: Intercity
highlight: Double-deck VIRM Intercitys run almost the whole length of the country to Maastricht, the old Dutch city on the Meuse near the Belgian border.

name: Intercity Direct
highlight: New ICNG trains take the HSL-Zuid high-speed line, hitting 200 km/h as they link Amsterdam, Rotterdam and Breda without a change.

name: Miljoenenlijn
highlight: Nicknamed the Million Line for its ruinously costly cuttings and viaducts, this steam railway climbs the only real hills in the Netherlands, in South Limburg.

name: Intercity
highlight: One of Belgium’s original 1838 trunk lines, running dead-flat across Flanders through Ghent and Bruges to the North Sea ferry port of Ostend.

name: Intercity
highlight: Terminates beneath Santiago Calatrava’s soaring white vault of steel and glass at Liège-Guillemins, one of Europe’s boldest modern stations.

name: Intercity
highlight: Climbs from Brussels through the forested Ardennes via Namur and Arlon; planned upgrades aim to cut the three-hour run to Luxembourg below two hours by 2030.

name: Intercity
highlight: Traces the route of continental Europe’s first passenger railway of 1835 and ends under the vast domed railway cathedral of Antwerpen-Centraal.

name: Kusttram
highlight: The world’s longest tram line: 67 km of metre-gauge track hugging the entire Belgian coast from De Panne to Knokke, calling at 68 stops.

name: Intercity
highlight: A cross-border Intercity through the wooded Éislek and the Ourthe and Amblève valleys, one of the prettiest rail rides in the Ardennes.

name: RE 11 Mosel
highlight: The RE 11 follows the Moselle from Luxembourg into Germany, boring through the 4.2 km Kaiser-Wilhelm-Tunnel at Cochem to bypass the river’s huge loop.

name: TER
highlight: A short cross-border hop from Luxembourg’s Ville-Haute down to the old French steel town of Longwy, one of the busiest daily commuter runs into the Grand Duchy.

name: Marschbahn
highlight: Reaches the car-free island of Sylt over the 11 km Hindenburgdamm causeway of 1927, a dead-straight embankment laid across the tidal Wadden Sea.

name: TGV inOui
highlight: France’s first high-speed line, opened in 1981: the Paris–Lyon route that launched the whole TGV era, now run in under two hours.

name: TGV inOui
highlight: The 2017 Bretagne–Pays de la Loire high-speed line cut Paris–Rennes to 1 h 25 min, sending TGVs racing across Brittany at 320 km/h.

name: TGV inOui
highlight: TGV Atlantique runs Paris to the Loire estuary city of Nantes in about two hours, topping 300 km/h across the plains of the west.

name: TGV Lyria
highlight: A Lyria Euroduplex glides from Paris into Switzerland at Genève-Cornavin; the 2010 Haut-Bugey line trimmed the run to about three hours.

name: Ouigo
highlight: SNCF’s low-cost double-decker TGV, launched in 2013 with fares from €10, races Paris to Lyon at up to 300 km/h.

name: TGV inOui
highlight: France’s longest domestic TGV run: 300 km/h to Marseille, then the classic Riviera line along the Mediterranean to Nice.

name: TGV inOui
highlight: The dead-straight LGV Nord links Paris to Lille in just 62 minutes at 300 km/h, France’s fastest big-city hop.

name: Intercités
highlight: The Bourbonnais line to the Auvergne volcanoes; new Oxygène trainsets arrive from 2026 to retire its veteran Corail carriages.

name: Intercités
highlight: The cross-country POLT line, Paris-Orléans-Limoges-Toulouse, runs 713 km through the Massif Central on France’s classic north–south artery.

name: Train des Pignes
highlight: Metre-gauge “pine-cone train” climbing 151 km from Nice to Digne over some 25 tunnels and 16 viaducts, inaugurated in 1911.

name: Train des Merveilles
highlight: Spirals up the wild Roya valley past the Bronze-Age rock carvings of the Vallée des Merveilles, looping through helicoidal tunnels to Tende at 1,040 m.

name: Mont-Blanc Express
highlight: A metre-gauge mountain line under Mont Blanc, using third-rail power and a rack section to climb the Chamonix valley from Saint-Gervais into Switzerland.

name: Chemins de fer de la Corse
highlight: Corsica’s beloved “U Trinichellu” crosses the island’s granite spine, piercing the 3.9 km Vizzavona tunnel at 906 m altitude.

name: AVE
highlight: Branches off the Madrid–Levante high-speed line to reach the Costa Blanca, opened in 2013 and worked by S-112 “Pato” trainsets.

name: AVE
highlight: The first stretch of Spain’s Madrid–Barcelona line (2003), crossing the Guadalajara plateau and Ebro valley to Zaragoza in 75 minutes.

name: AVE
highlight: Leaps into Galicia through 30 tunnels, including the 8.6 km O Corno tunnel, on the mountain-defying line opened in December 2021.

name: AVE
highlight: Opened in 2019, it branches off the Córdoba–Málaga line at Antequera to lift the AVE network into the foothills of the Sierra Nevada.

name: Avlo
highlight: Renfe’s low-cost AVE, launched June 2021 with fares from €7, blitzes Madrid–Barcelona at 330 km/h aboard Avril trainsets.

name: AVE
highlight: A single high-speed run tying the Mediterranean to the Costa del Sol, skirting Madrid to stitch two AVE corridors together.

name: El Transcantábrico
highlight: Spain’s first hotel-train (1983): an eight-day, metre-gauge cruise along the green Cantabrian coast from the Basque Country to Galicia.

name: Tren dels Llacs
highlight: A heritage “train of the lakes” climbing beside the Noguera Pallaresa past reservoirs and tunnels to the Pyrenean foothills.

name: El Expreso de La Robla
highlight: Retraces the historic La Robla coal railway, Spain’s longest narrow-gauge line, on a three-day luxury run between Bilbao and León.

name: Alfa Pendular
highlight: Portugal’s tilting Pendolino leans into the curves at up to 220 km/h, dropping down the Alentejo to the Algarve at Faro.

name: Intercidades
highlight: Climbs the Beira Alta line to Guarda, Portugal’s highest city at 1,056 m, on the historic Sud Express path toward the Spanish border.

name: Presidential Train
highlight: Royal-blue saloon carriages built from 1890, which once carried heads of state, now a fine-dining train up the terraced Douro wine valley.

name: Linha do Minho
highlight: Follows the Atlantic, then the Minho river, to the star-fort town of Valença, staring across the water at Spain’s Tui.

name: Linha do Oeste
highlight: A slow, winding single-track line through the vineyards and spa towns of Estremadura (Torres Vedras, Caldas da Rainha) to the Atlantic at Figueira da Foz.

name: Frecciarossa
highlight: A Frecciarossa 1000 hit 393.8 km/h on this Turin–Milan high-speed line in a 2016 test, an Italian rail speed record.

name: Frecciarossa
highlight: The 300 km/h spine of Italy. Milan–Bologna–Florence–Rome–Naples runs almost entirely on dedicated high-speed track finished in 2009.

name: Frecciarossa
highlight: South of Caserta the Freccia climbs through the Apennines to Foggia, then races across the Tavoliere plain to the Adriatic port of Bari.

name: Frecciargento
highlight: One of Italy’s longest Freccia runs: some 671 km down the Tyrrhenian coast, the tilting ETR 485 leaning through curves toward the Strait of Messina.

name: Italo
highlight: Italo was Europe’s first private open-access high-speed operator; its Alstom AGV 575 links Milan and Rome in under three hours.

name: Frecciarossa
highlight: A tilting ETR 600 Frecciarossa crosses the Po plain and Veneto from Milan to Trieste on the Adriatic, a run it took over in June 2023.

name: Ferrovia Circumetnea
highlight: A 950 mm narrow-gauge line almost encircling Mount Etna, Europe’s most active volcano, crossing black lava fields around Bronte and Randazzo.

name: Trenino Verde
highlight: At 159 km the longest tourist railway in Italy, this narrow-gauge line climbs to 868 m through the wild Ogliastra mountains to the sea at Arbatax.

name: Rittner Bahn
highlight: A metre-gauge survivor of a 1907 rack railway, its vintage tramcars glide across the sunny Renon plateau above Bolzano, the Dolomites on the horizon.

name: Circumvesuviana
highlight: The narrow-gauge line to Sorrento, opened in 1948, carries travellers past the ruins of Pompeii and Herculaneum under the shadow of Vesuvius.

name: Pelion Railway
highlight: Evaristo de Chirico’s 600 mm mountain railway on Mt Pelion curls over his iron-and-stone bridge near Milies through olive and apple groves.

name: Proastiakos
highlight: Athens’s fast electric suburban line skirts the Saronic Gulf and crosses the Corinth Canal on its run from the port of Piraeus out to Kiato.

name: Zagreb–Rijeka line
highlight: Climbs through the forested Gorski Kotar highlands, through tunnels and curves before the steep final plunge to the Adriatic at Rijeka.

name: Zagreb–Osijek line
highlight: Runs east across the flat Pannonian farmland of Slavonia to Osijek, Croatia’s fourth-largest city, on the Drava near the Hungarian border.

name: Ljubljana–Koper line
highlight: Drops nearly 500 m off the Karst plateau to the sea, twisting through tunnels and curves on the descent to Slovenia’s only seaport, Koper.

name: Podgorica–Nikšić line
highlight: Reopened to passengers after electrification in 2012, the line takes 12 tunnels and 9 bridges below the cliffs of Ostrog Monastery.

name: Sarajevo–Mostar line
highlight: Threads the plunging Neretva canyon between the Prenj and Čvrsnica massifs: the line onward to the Adriatic bores through 106 tunnels.

name: Skopje–Bitola line
highlight: Climbs from the Vardar at Veles over the Babuna mountains on the looping Bogomila grade before dropping onto the Pelagonia plain to Bitola.

name: Skopje–Gevgelija line
highlight: Follows the Vardar south and squeezes through the Demir Kapija, the “Iron Gate” gorge, on Corridor X’s run toward Greece.

name: Pristina–Peja line
highlight: Kosovo’s sole surviving passenger service: two little diesel trains a day amble west via Klina to Peja under the Accursed Mountains.

name: Frecciarossa
highlight: Frecciarossa 1000 sets cross from Rome over the Apennines and the Po plain via Florence and Bologna to reach Venice in under four hours.

name: Transiberiana d’Italia
highlight: Italy’s “Trans-Siberian”: a heritage train over snowbound Apennine plateaus to Rivisondoli-Pescocostanzo, at 1,268 m among the country’s highest stations.

name: Ferrovia delle Meraviglie
highlight: The “Railway of Wonders” spirals up the Alps through helical tunnels and viaducts into France; wrecked in WWII, it reopened only in 1979.

name: Vinschgau Railway
highlight: Closed to passengers in 1990, this South Tyrol branch reopened in 2005 as a rural-rail success story and went fully electric in 2026.

name: Frecciargento
highlight: A tilting ETR 485 runs Rome to the heel of Italy’s boot via Foggia, Bari and Brindisi, reaching baroque Lecce in the Salento.

name: InterCity Slovenija
highlight: SŽ’s tilting Pendolino links Slovenia’s two largest cities along the 1840s Austrian Southern Railway, weaving down the Sava and Savinja valleys.

name: Narita Express
highlight: Twin sets from Yokohama and Shinjuku couple at Tokyo Station, then run as one red-and-black E259 through the Sobu tunnels out to Narita Airport.

name: Thunderbird
highlight: Since the 2024 bullet-train extension it sprints from Osaka up the wild western shore of Lake Biwa to Tsuruga, Hokuriku’s new shinkansen gateway.

name: Haruka
highlight: Kansai Airport’s purple-and-white express, covering 99 km from Kyoto’s temples out across the bay to the man-made island runway.

name: Kaiji
highlight: Chuo Line express using the E353’s active tilt to swing through the mountain-walled Katsura valleys up to the vineyard capital of Kofu.

name: Hokuto
highlight: Diesel tilting express curving 319 km around Uchiura Bay past the volcanoes Usu and Komagatake, linking Sapporo with old Hakodate.

name: Ozora
highlight: Crosses Hokkaido’s wild interior, the Hidaka range and the Sekisho tunnels, to the crane marshes of Kushiro on the far Pacific coast.

name: Enoden
highlight: Century-old seaside interurban that skims Shonan’s surf beaches and brushes past house walls; its Kamakurakokomae crossing is an anime shrine.

name: Oigawa SL Kawane-ji
highlight: Vintage C11 steam locomotives haul old coaches up the Oi River valley: the same railway that runs Japan’s Thomas the Tank Engine trains.

name: Isumi Railway
highlight: Restored 1960s diesel railcars trundle past rice paddies and spring nanohana blossoms through the rural heart of the Boso Peninsula.

name: Shimakaze
highlight: Kintetsu’s luxury Shimakaze (double-decker observation cars, leather saloons and a café) glides to the pearl bays and shrines of Ise-Shima.

name: Laview
highlight: Seibu’s radical Laview, with a huge curved windscreen and yellow living-room windows, climbs from Ikebukuro into the Chichibu mountains.

name: Fujisan View Express
